annotations.label: Sturm–Liouville problem

anchor.snippet: a Sturm–Liouville problem is a second-order linear ordinary differential equation of the form

annotations.note: Grep for `Sturm` in `Mathlib/` returns no Sturm–Liouville definitions.

annotations.label: Eigenvalues of a Sturm–Liouville problem

anchor.snippet: are called the eigenvalues of the problem

annotations.note: Mathlib has general `Module.End.HasEigenvalue`, but no Sturm–Liouville eigenvalue definition.

annotations.label: Eigenfunctions of a Sturm–Liouville problem

anchor.snippet: are called the eigenfunctions associated to each

annotations.note: No Sturm–Liouville eigenfunction definition appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sturm–Liouville theory

anchor.snippet: Sturm–Liouville theory is the general study of Sturm–Liouville problems

annotations.note: The topic itself is absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Regular Sturm–Liouville problem

anchor.snippet: The problem is said to be regular if

annotations.note: No regularity definition for Sturm–Liouville problems is present.

annotations.label: Weight (density) function

anchor.snippet: is called the weight or density function

annotations.note: No SL weight-function concept present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Solution of a regular Sturm–Liouville problem

anchor.snippet: is called a solution if it is continuously differentiable

annotations.note: No SL solution notion present.

annotations.label: Main theorem of Sturm–Liouville theory

anchor.snippet: The main result of Sturm–Liouville theory states that

annotations.note: The SL main theorem is not stated in Mathlib; only a finite-dimensional symmetric-operator spectral theorem exists in `Mathlib/Analysis/InnerProductSpace/Spectrum.lean`.

annotations.label: No nontrivial solution has infinitely many zeros on a closed interval

anchor.snippet: there are no non-trivial solutions to the above equation which have infinitely many zeros

annotations.note: No Mathlib statement about zeros of ODE solutions.

annotations.note: Sturm's separation theorem is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sturm's Fundamental Theorem

annotations.note: Sturm's comparison/fundamental theorem is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sturm–Liouville form (self-adjoint form)

anchor.snippet: is said to be in Sturm–Liouville form or self-adjoint form

annotations.note: The Sturm–Liouville canonical form of a second-order ODE is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.note: The reduction lemma is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.note: The Bessel equation itself is not in Mathlib, let alone its SL form.

annotations.note: The Legendre differential equation is not present in Mathlib (only Legendre symbols/polynomials in algebraic contexts).

annotations.label: Reduction via integrating factor

anchor.snippet: Multiplying throughout by an integrating factor

annotations.note: Integrating-factor SL reduction examples are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Integrating factor for general second-order homogeneous equation

anchor.section: Integrating factor for general second-order homogeneous equation
anchor.snippet: Multiplying through by the integrating factor

annotations.label: Sturm–Liouville operator L

anchor.section: Sturm–Liouville equations as self-adjoint differential operators
anchor.snippet: can be viewed as a linear operator L mapping a function u to another function Lu

annotations.note: No SL operator is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: L is a self-adjoint operator

anchor.section: Sturm–Liouville equations as self-adjoint differential operators
anchor.snippet: L is a self-adjoint operator

annotations.note: The specific self-adjointness of the SL operator is not stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Eigenvalues of L are real; eigenfunctions with different eigenvalues are orthogonal

anchor.section: Sturm–Liouville equations as self-adjoint differential operators
anchor.snippet: the eigenvalues of a Sturm–Liouville operator are real and that eigenfunctions of L corresponding to different eigenvalues are orthogonal

annotations.note: Mathlib proves this for any symmetric linear map (`conj_eigenvalue_eq_self` and `orthogonalFamily_eigenspaces`), but not instantiated for the SL operator since that operator is absent.

annotations.label: Resolvent is a compact integral operator; spectral theorem yields orthonormal basis of eigenfunctions

anchor.section: Sturm–Liouville equations as self-adjoint differential operators
anchor.snippet: the resolvent is an integral operator with a continuous symmetric kernel

annotations.note: Mathlib has a finite-dimensional symmetric-operator eigenbasis; the compact-resolvent Sturm–Liouville argument itself is not formalized.

annotations.label: Singular Sturm–Liouville operator

anchor.section: Sturm–Liouville equations as self-adjoint differential operators
anchor.snippet: one calls L singular

annotations.note: The singular-vs-regular SL distinction is not defined in Mathlib.

anchor.snippet: this can be reduced to the Sturm–Liouville form

annotations.label: Picard–Lindelöf existence and uniqueness for initial value problems

anchor.section: Application to inhomogeneous second-order boundary value problems
anchor.snippet: the Picard–Lindelöf theorem ensures that the differential equation has a unique solution

annotations.note: Picard–Lindelöf existence (plus uniqueness via `ODE_solution_unique`) is formalized in `Mathlib.Analysis.ODE`.

annotations.label: Solution of inhomogeneous problem via eigenfunction expansion

anchor.section: Application to inhomogeneous second-order boundary value problems
anchor.snippet: a large class of functions f can be expanded in terms of a series of orthonormal eigenfunctions

annotations.note: Eigenfunction-expansion solution of an inhomogeneous SL BVP is not in Mathlib.
